description: Barbell and bodyweight strength training grounded in progressive overload. Builds maximal strength, hypertrophy, and movement quality through compound lifts.

movements.name: Barbell Back Squat

commonMistakes.description: Allowing the knees to track inward (valgus collapse) during the ascent, placing high shear stress on the ACL and medial collateral ligament.
Performing a butt-wink by allowing the pelvis to posteriorly tilt at the bottom of the squat, which flexes the lumbar spine under load and increases disc injury risk.

movements.description: The foundational lower body exercise loading the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings through a full range of motion. Proper form demands a braced core and knees tracking over the toes throughout.

movements.name: Conventional Deadlift

commonMistakes.description: Jerking the bar off the floor instead of building tension gradually, causing the lower back to round before the legs can engage.
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the pull, increasing the moment arm on the lumbar spine and dramatically raising injury risk.

movements.description: A full-body pulling movement that develops posterior chain strength from the traps to the calves. Among the most effective exercises for building overall strength and muscle mass.

movements.name: Barbell Bench Press

commonMistakes.description: Flaring the elbows to 90 degrees relative to the torso, which places excessive anterior shear on the glenohumeral joint and strains the rotator cuff.
Bouncing the bar off the chest to overcome the sticking point, reducing time under tension and risking rib or sternum injury.

movements.description: The primary horizontal pressing exercise for developing the chest, anterior deltoid, and triceps. The standard benchmark of upper body pressing strength across gyms worldwide.

movements.name: Pull-Up

commonMistakes.description: Using a kipping or swinging motion to generate momentum, removing tension from the lats and placing sudden rotational stress on the shoulder joint at the top.
Not achieving full scapular depression and retraction before pulling, causing the upper trapezius to dominate and reducing lat engagement.

movements.description: A bodyweight vertical pulling exercise that builds the latissimus dorsi and biceps through a full overhead range. A reliable measure of upper body pulling strength relative to bodyweight.

movements.name: Barbell Overhead Press

commonMistakes.description: Hyperextending the lumbar spine to push the chest forward under heavy load, compensating for limited thoracic mobility and compressing the lumbar discs.

Pressing the bar in a straight vertical path rather than slightly back behind the head, preventing the bar from traveling over the body's center of gravity and making the lift mechanically inefficient.

movements.description: A vertical pressing movement that develops the deltoids, triceps, and upper trapezius. Requires active core engagement to prevent the lower back from hyperextending under load.

movements.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row

commonMistakes.description: Jerking the weight up with the lower back rather than initiating the pull with the elbows, risking spinal injury.
Allowing the torso to rise above 45 degrees during the pull, converting it into a partial deadlift.

movements.description: A horizontal pulling movement that builds thickness across the upper and mid back. The hinge setup mirrors the deadlift position, reinforcing posterior chain engagement patterns.

movements.name: Parallel Bar Dip

commonMistakes.description: Flaring the elbows outward excessively, placing the shoulder joint in an impingement-prone position.
Not reaching full lockout at the top, reducing the stimulus to the triceps.

movements.description: A compound pushing exercise using parallel bars to target the triceps, chest, and anterior deltoid. Forward lean increases chest involvement while an upright torso focuses effort on the triceps.

movements.name: Romanian Deadlift

commonMistakes.description: Bending the knees too much and converting the movement into a conventional deadlift, missing the hamstring stretch.
Rounding the upper back instead of maintaining a neutral spine throughout the hinge.

movements.description: A hip-hinge movement that places maximum eccentric tension on the hamstrings throughout the lowering phase. The bar stays above the knee, distinguishing it from a conventional deadlift.

movements.name: Barbell Front Squat

commonMistakes.description: Allowing the elbows to drop during the descent, which tips the bar forward off the shoulders and forces the torso to follow, destroying the upright posture that front squats require.
Using a cross-arm grip instead of a clean grip when wrist flexibility allows, which provides less bar control and is more likely to unbalance the load.

movements.description: A squat variation with the bar resting on the front deltoids, demanding greater quad dominance and thoracic extension than the back squat. The upright torso position is more spine-friendly under load.

movements.name: Barbell Hip Thrust

commonMistakes.description: Hyperextending the lumbar spine at the top to achieve greater range, loading the lower back instead of finishing with glute contraction at a neutral spine position.
Positioning the bench too low, which forces the shoulders onto the edge at an uncomfortable angle and prevents the hip from reaching full extension.

movements.description: A glute-isolation exercise loading the gluteus maximus at the peak of hip extension. The horizontal force vector at full contraction makes this uniquely effective for posterior development.

movements.name: Seated Cable Row

commonMistakes.description: Using excessive trunk lean-back to initiate the pull, which recruits the erector spinae to move the weight rather than the target muscles and risks low-back strain.
Not fully extending the arms before each rep, shortening the range of motion and reducing the lat stretch that maximizes the training stimulus.

movements.description: A seated horizontal pull with consistent tension throughout the full range of motion, unlike free weights. The cable ensures the muscles are loaded both at full stretch and at peak contraction.

movements.name: Cable Face Pull

commonMistakes.description: Pulling the rope to the chin rather than the forehead, which reduces external rotation and defeats the primary purpose of the exercise as a rotator cuff and rear delt movement.
Using too much weight and compensating with trunk extension, turning the face pull into a partial row and losing the isolated external rotation component.

movements.description: A rear deltoid and external rotator exercise using a rope attachment pulled toward the face. Corrects the internal rotation pattern caused by heavy pressing and maintains long-term shoulder health.
